coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive chemiluminescence assay for 4-nitrophenol by using luminol–NaIO4 reaction catalyzed by copper, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Delnavaz, Elnaz
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: A chemiluminescence (CL) assay on the basis of the tremendous enhancing effect of copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) on the luminol–NaIO4 reaction was introduced for the determination of nanomolar levels of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Cu,N-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal approach and characterized by TEM, XRD, EDX, and FTIR analysis. The potential CL mechanism was elucidated by recording the CL spectrum and by evaluating the influence of reactive oxygen species. It was found that 4-NP remarkably inhibited the luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs reaction and reduced the CL signal of the reaction. This fact was applied for developing a CL assay for 4-NP. Under the optimized conditions, 4-NP could be determined in the concentration range of 0.25 to 150 nM, with a detection limit as low as 0.06 nM. This assay was successfully exploited for the analysis of 4-NP in real environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-doped carbon dots
Carbon nanomaterials
Nitrophenol
Toxic compounds
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Beahvior of Cu,N-CDs in the chemiluminescence
Analytical performance of the CL probe for 4-NP assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu,N-CDs. Insert: Photograph of Cu,N-CDs under the excitation of UV light with 365 nm (a) excitation (λem = 442 nm) and emission (λex = 370 nm) spectra of N, Cu-CDs (b).
TEM image (a), FT-IR spectrum (b), XRD spectrum (c), and EDX spectrum of Cu,N-CDs (d).

Kinetic profile of luminol–NaIO4 (A) luminol–NaIO4–undoped CDs (B), luminol–NaIO4–N-CDs (C), luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs reaction (D). conditions: luminol (0.05 μM), NaIO4 (0.1 mM), NaOH (2.5 mM), undoped CDs (100 μL), N-CDs (100 μL), and Cu,N-CDs (50 μL).

CL spectra of luminol-NaIO4 CL reaction in basic medium in the absence; NaOH (1.0 M) and NaIO4 (0.001 M) in one line and luminol (0.0001 M) in other line (a) and in the presence of N, Cu-CDs; NaOH (1.0 M) and NaIO4 (0.001 M) in one line and luminol (0.0001 M) and Cu,N-CDs solution and in other line (b).
Time profiles for luminol-NaIO4-Cu,N-CDs CL reaction in the optimum conditions and in the presence of different concentration of 4-NP (a) and corresponding calibration plot (b).
The effect of various phenolic compounds with concentration of 10 nM on CL intensity of luminol–NaIO4-Cu,N-CDs reaction.
Influence of various radical scavengers on Cu,N-CDs–luminol–NaIO4 CL reaction in the absence and presence of 4-NP (50 nM).
Comparison of the performance of different methods for the determination of 4-NP.
Results for the analysis of 4-NP in real environmental samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive chemiluminescence assay for 4-nitrophenol by using luminol–NaIO4 reaction catalyzed by copper, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A chemiluminescence (CL) assay on the basis of the tremendous enhancing effect of copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) on the luminol–NaIO4 reaction was introduced for the determination of nanomolar levels of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Cu,N-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal approach and characterized by TEM, XRD, EDX, and FTIR analysis. The potential CL mechanism was elucidated by recording the CL spectrum and by evaluating the influence of reactive oxygen species. It was found that 4-NP remarkably inhibited the luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs reaction and reduced the CL signal of the reaction. This fact was applied for developing a CL assay for 4-NP. Under the optimized conditions, 4-NP could be determined in the concentration range of 0.25 to 150 nM, with a detection limit as low as 0.06 nM. This assay was successfully exploited for the analysis of 4-NP in real environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols (NPs) are identified as sorely toxic compounds due to their adverse impacts on human health and the environment even at trace amounts [1]. These compounds are very persistent and can remain unchanged in the soil and groundwater for a long time due to their gradual degradation by microorganisms [2]. NPs have been discovered in the environment in growing quantities because of their widespread usage in the manufacturing of dyestuffs, plastics, adhesives, drugs, pesticides, and explosives [1–3]. 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is one of the harmful and refractory nitrophenol isomers, which is very soluble in aquatic environments [4]. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has cited 4-NP on priority containment list because of its dermatological and possible carcinogenic effects on human beings [1,2,5]. The permissible concentration of 4-NP in water was set to 60 μg L−1 by EPA [6]. Thus, it is crucial to establish sensitive, facile, cost-effective, fast, and precise methods for the assay of 4-NP. Over the last decade, various techniques for the quantitative measurement of 4-NP in environmental samples have been introduced such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], electrochemistry [8], fluorescence [4], colorimetric [5], and chemiluminescence [9].

Chemiluminescence (CL) is the generation of electromagnetic emission from an excited reagent, which arises from a redox reaction among the reagents [10]. CL is recognized as a powerful analytical method that exhibits a variety of advantages including excellent sensitivity, a very low limit of detection, the facility of operation, cost-effective instruments and short response time [11]. Therefore, CL assays have engrossed tremendous interest among the researchers. Recently, nanostructured carbon dots (CDs) have been widely exploited in CL reactions to enhance their sensitivity [12,13]. CDs are recognized as the promising carbon nanomaterials that enticed scientific attention in diverse areas, which results from their eminent photochemical and physicochemical features and facile and cost-effective synthesis procedures [14,15]. Various strategies such as metal ions doping have been adopted to improve the performance and broaden the applications of CDs. Doping of metal atoms into CDs is an efficient way to induce novel intrinsic properties such as catalytic performance and improve the optical characteristics [16,17]. For example, metal ions can act as passivation agents by preserving the carboxyl or carbonyl groups on the surface of the CDs, which lead to enhancement of the photoluminescence quantum yield [16,18–23]. Also, the interference effect of metal ions that exist in a real sample can reduce in the fluorescence measurements by using metal ion-doped CDs [24]. Lin et al. [25] proposed a colorimetric probe on the basis of the peroxidase-like activity of copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Cu,N-CDs) for discrimination and determination of phenylenediamine isomers. Xu et al. [17] reported highly fluorescent zinc-doped CDs for monitoring of H2O2 and glucose based on the Fenton reaction. Nevertheless, we found only one report on the use of metal ion-doped CDs as catalysts in the CL field [26].

In this paper, we assessed the catalytic performance of Cu,N-CDs in the CL reaction of luminol–NaIO4 compared to other kinds of CDs. It was observed that the addition of Cu,N-CDs substantially amplified the CL signal of the system in the basic medium. Moreover, it was shown that ultra-trace amounts of 4-NP exhibited an inhibiting effect on the CL emission. Thus an ultrasensitive CL nanoprobe was constructed for the monitoring of 4-NP in water samples.

The CL experimentations were carried out on a LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold, Germany; www.berthold.com), which have an automatic injector. Fluorescence and CL spectra were acquired on a Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan; www.jascoinc.com). The absorption spectrum was conducted on a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). The characterization of the prepared Cu,N-CDs were performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany; www.zeiss.com), X–ray diffraction (XRD, D5000, Siemens, USA, the radiation source was Cu Kα), FT-IR spectrometry (Bruker Co., Germany; www.bruker.com) and energy dispersive X–ray (EDX, MIRA III, France; www.tescan.com) spectroscopy.

Copper acetate monohydrate, citric acid monohydrate, diethylenetriamine, NaIO4, NaOH, luminol, and 4-NP were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany; www.merckmillipore.com). The stock solution of NaIO4 (0.001 M) was prepared daily. Luminol stock solution (0.001 M) was made by dissolving a proper amount of luminol in 10 mL of 0.01 M NaOH solution and kept in the dark place at 4 °C. A 0.001 M stock solution of 4-NP was prepared in deionized water. Deionized water supplied by aqua MAX- Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin Instrument Co., Korea) was applied during this work.
Cu,N-CDs, N-CDs, and un-doped CDs were prepared based on the previously reported method [24]. The details of procedure are given in the Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

Firstly, a proper volume of 4-NP standard solution was transferred into a 3-mL CL cuvette, then 50 μL of diluted Cu,N-CDs, 100 μL of NaIO4 (0.001 M), and 50 μL of luminol (1.0 μM) were orderly added into the cuvette and the final volume was adjusted to 950 μL with deionized water. Afterward, the CL reaction was initiated by injection of 50 μL of NaOH (0.05 M), and simultaneously, the CL signal was measured. The highest CL intensity was chosen for subsequent CL measurements.

Water samples were firstly filtrated with a filter paper (ALBET, DP 135 125, Barcelona, Spain) to dispel particulate matters. The samples were passed through a cation exchanger to eliminate the major metal cations and prevent their interference. Finally, the appropriate volume of each sample was utilized for 4-NP assay according to the method in Section 2.4. For recovery experiments, the water samples were spiked with certain amounts of the 4-NP standard solution before any pretreatment.

Optical properties of the Cu,N-CDs were evaluated by fluorescence spectra as well as UV–Vis absorption. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1a) exhibited two absorption peaks at about 240 and 350 nm, resulting from the π–π* transition of CC and the n–π* transition of CO bonds, respectively. Under the excitation of UV light with 365 nm wavelength, the hazel solution of Cu,N-CDs emitted a bright blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 1a). The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 370 and 442 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b).

The TEM image indicated that Cu,N-CDs were almost monodispersed spherical dots with size distribution below 10 nm (Fig. 2a). FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b) was utilized to inspect the structural properties of Cu,N-CDs. The absorption peaks at 3426, 1708, 1435, and 1215 cm−1 were ascribed to the OH, CO, CN, and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks appeared at 2935, 764, 676 cm−1 were related to the CH bonds vibrations. The CC vibrational bonds were centered at 1650, 1552 cm−1. The bonds at the 920–1000 cm−1 region were attributed to the stretching vibration of N-Cu-N [24,27]. Furthermore, the peak at around 530 cm−1 was related to the vibrational stretching of CuO [28]. Therefore, these results demonstrated that copper was present in Cu,N-CDs. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) displayed a broad diffraction peak located at 2θ =20°, representing the graphitic structure of the Cu,N-CDs [29]. No obvious peak of Cu was observed in the XRD pattern, which probably is due to the low amount of Cu content in CD matrix. Moreover, the EDX spectrum (Fig. 2d) approved the existence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and copper in the Cu,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Beahvior of Cu,N-CDs in the chemiluminescence
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of Cu,N-CDs as well as un-doped CDs and N-CDs on the luminol-NaIO4 CL system in the basic medium was evaluated. The results (Fig. 3 ) showed that un-doped CDs did not have a considerable effect on the CL intensity, but Cu,N-CDs could superbly enhance (about 280-fold) the CL intensity in comparison with N-CDs (about 90-fold). Therefore we applied Cu,N-CDs as an amplifier of the signal for the subsequent CL assays. The CL reaction was also investigated in the presence of components (i.e. copper acetate monohydrate, citric acid monohydrate and diethylenetriamine) with the same concentrations as utilized in the synthesis of Cu,N-CDs. Since no sensible results were observed for these solutions, the promoting effect was attributable to the Cu,N-CDs.

In the next step, the possible mechanism of CL generation was studied by specifying the emitting species. The CL spectrum with a clear emission maximum at 425 nm was acquired by a spectrofluorimeter (Fig. 4 ). Hence, it was deduced that the CL emitter both in the absence and presence of Cu,N-CDs was 3-aminophthalate, which is known as the oxidation product of luminol. It means that the enhancement of CL intensity could be ascribed to the catalytic property of Cu,N-CDs. In the luminol-NaIO4 CL, reactive oxygen species (such OH as and O2
− radicals) were produced in the reaction of dissolved O2 in basic solution and periodate, and these radicals acted as an oxidizing agent for the luminol [30,31]. In order to affirm the role of oxygen species in the CL reaction, a set of experiments in the presence of various kinds of radical scavengers were performed. As shown in Table 1 , these scavengers resulted in a diminishing effect on the CL emission of the reaction. This demonstrated that oxygen radicals were deeply involved in the reaction. The influence of the dissolved oxygen on the CL system was also evaluated. When the solution was deoxygenated with bubbling Ar gas, the intensity of the CL reaction was reduced by about 72%. Therefore, it was proved that the dissolved oxygen had a significant role in the CL generation.

As mentioned in the literature [32], CDs could act as electron donors and the existence of valence electrons in the dopant metal ions could enhance the electron-donating performance of the CDs [21,27]. Therefore, it could be inferred that the enhanced electron-donating performance of the Cu,N-CDs could accelerate the production of reactive oxygen species, which caused to facilitate the oxidation of luminol and so, amplified the CL emission of the system. We found that DMSO (3.0 mM) and ascorbic acid (0.5 mM) as scavengers of OH and O2
− radicals have much less effect on luminol–NaIO4 reaction compared to luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs system (62.9 and 97.5% quenching, respectively, compared to 96.6 and 99.9% in the presence of Cu,N-CDs). This indicates that more reactive oxygen species are produced in the presence of Cu,N-CDs.

To acquire the maximum catalytic property of Cu,N-CDs, the influence of diverse factors such as the concentration of NaOH, NaIO4, luminol, and amount of Cu,N-CDs were assessed on the CL system. The results were given in the Electronic Supplementary Materials (Figs. S1–S4, ESM). Based on these results, 0.05 μM luminol, 0.1 mM NaIO4, 2.5 mM NaOH and 50 μL Cu,N-CDs were selected as the optimum values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CL probe for 4-NP assay
ce:section.ce:para: As represented in Fig. 5 , upon the addition of ultra-trace amounts of 4-NP the CL signal of luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs system was reduced. In order to gain insight into the mechanism of this inhibition, the influence of various scavengers on the CL signal in the presence of 4-NP was evaluated. It was evident from the results (Table 1) that the scavengers had a lower quenching effect than the situation in which no 4-NP was used. Also, deoxygenation of the solution in the presence of 50 nM 4-NP, caused a lesser reduction (about 30%) in the CL intensity contrasted to that in the absence of 4-NP. These results showed that the presence of 4-NP diminished the production of reactive oxygen species. The electron-withdrawing character of 4-NP may lead to the transfer of the electrons from Cu,N-CDs to 4-NP. This interaction could decline the Cu,N-CDs ability as the electron donor, which caused a reduction in the catalytic performance of Cu,N-CDs and thus decreased the amounts of reactive oxygen species.

According to these observations, sensitive detection of 4-NP through the CL probe was accomplished. The CL signal was linearly reduced for the 4-NP concentrations from 0.25 to 150 nM with a detection limit of 0.06 nM (S/N = 3). The related linear equation was logΔI = 0.2793 logC +4.7936, R2 = 0.9973, where ΔI = I0-I is the difference between the CL signal in the presence (I) and in the absence (I0) of 4-NP, and C is the concentration of 4-NP in nM (Fig. 5). The relative standard deviation (RSD%) was calculated to be 2.3% for five repetitive measurements of 50 nM 4-NP, which demonstrated the good precision of the probe. Comparing the characteristics of this CL assay with some other methods for 4-NP determination (Table 2 ), exhibited that this method had a better linear dynamic range with much lower detection limit than most fluorescent and CL methods reported.

The effect of various phenolic compounds including nitrophenols and chlorophenols on the CL of luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs reaction was evaluated and the results were graphically displayed in Fig. 6 . As can be seen, the CL probe had higher response for 4-NP, which was probably due to its stronger electron-withdrawing ability compared to other phenolic compounds. Moreover, the interference effect of various common ions on the determination of 50 nM of 4-NP was studied. The tolerance limit for each components in the relative error of <5% are shown in Table S1 (ESM).

The performance of the CL sensor was appraised for the determination of 4-NP in real wastewater and spring water samples. The obtained results are presented in Table 3 . It was observed that the recoveries of 4-NP ranged from 96 to 107%, which proved the good accuracy of this CL probe for 4-NP analysis. The Student t-test affirmed that there were no significant differences between the added and measured values. The results indicated that this sensitized CL assay for 4-NP could be employed for the analysis of environmental samples.

In conclusion, an ultrasensitive CL assay was introduced for the analysis of 4-NP based on its inhibiting effect on the luminol–NaIO4–Cu,N-CDs CL reaction. It was shown that Cu,N-CDs could effectively promote the production of reactive oxygen species, which facilitate the oxidation of luminol and finally intensified the CL signal. We observed that the CL emission of the reaction was declined with the addition of 4-NP, probably because of its interaction with Cu,N-CDs and decreasing the generation of reactive oxygen species. The probe showed and an excellent limit of detection and good precision for the analysis of 4-NP in real environmental samples.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Elnaz Delnavaz:Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft.Mohammad Amjadi:Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118608.
